description: Chess opening coaching knowledge base for Chess Opening Trainer. Chapters teach popular openings with ideas, plans, and practical advice — not deep memorization. Use chapter titles in the system prompt; fetch full content with a get-chapter-by-title tool.

chapters.title: Opening Principles
chapters.content:
Core goals of the opening (first ~10–15 moves):

1. Control the center. The squares e4, d4, e5, and d5 are the most valuable real estate. Occupy them with pawns when safe, or aim pieces at them. Central control gives space and mobility.

2. Develop knights and bishops early. Get minor pieces off the back rank to useful squares. A useful rule of thumb: knights before bishops — knight best squares (f3/c3 or f6/c6) are usually obvious; bishop placement depends on the opponent.

3. Castle early (ideally by move 10). A king left in the center is a target for tactics. Castling also connects the rooks so they can occupy open or semi-open files.

4. Do not move the same piece twice without a concrete reason. Tempo matters. Every extra move with one piece is a move your other pieces did not get.

5. Do not bring the queen out too early. An early queen is often chased by minor pieces, and you lose tempi while the opponent develops for free.

6. Do not grab side pawns while undeveloped. Development and king safety beat greedy pawn grabs in the opening.

7. Connect your ideas. After developing, ask: What is my pawn break? Where do my pieces want to go? What is my opponent trying to do?

Practical coaching tip: Under roughly 1500 rating, following these principles well is more important than memorizing long theoretical lines. Correct a student gently when they violate a principle, and name which principle was broken.

chapters.title: How to Study Openings
chapters.content:
Study openings by understanding, not by rote memorization.

What to learn at beginner / early intermediate level:
- About 5–8 moves of a main line for each opening you play
- The typical plans and pawn breaks after those moves
- A few common traps and how to avoid them
- The pawn structures that arise and what they suggest in the middlegame

What NOT to do:
- Memorize 20+ moves without knowing why
- Switch openings every week
- Rely only on engine first lines without human explanations

Effective study loop:
1. Learn the main idea of the opening in plain language.
2. Play through a short main line move by move, asking "why?" for each move.
3. Play practice games with that opening.
4. Review losses: where did you leave a principle or miss a typical plan?
5. Deepen only the branches you actually meet.

Repertoire size for beginners:
- One system as White (e.g. Italian or London)
- One reply to 1.e4 as Black (e.g. 1...e5 or Caro-Kann)
- One reply to 1.d4 as Black (e.g. Queen's Gambit Declined structures or a simple setup)

Consistency beats breadth. Stick with a small repertoire until the middlegame plans feel familiar.

When coaching: prefer explaining the idea and the next recommended move over dumping long theory trees.

chapters.title: Building a Beginner Repertoire
chapters.content:
A repertoire is the set of openings you choose to play regularly.

Suggested beginner-friendly pairs by style:

Tactical / open play:
- White: Italian Game (1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4)
- Black vs 1.e4: 1...e5 (learning both sides of Open Games)
- Black vs 1.d4: something clear and classical (e.g. ...d5 systems)

Solid / positional play:
- White: London System (1.d4 with Bf4 setup)
- Black vs 1.e4: Caro-Kann (1...c6)
- Black vs 1.d4: Queen's Gambit Declined ideas (...d5 and ...e6)

Educational classical path (matches this app's lessons):
- White: Italian Game and/or Ruy Lopez; also Queen's Gambit and London
- Facing Sicilian and Caro-Kann as White so you learn both sides of popular Black defenses

Practical advice:
- Pick openings you enjoy; motivation matters.
- Prefer openings with clear plans over trendy sharp lines.
- Learn the same opening from both colors when possible — it doubles understanding.
- Expand repertoire only after you can explain your opening's goals in one or two sentences.

This app's lessons cover: Italian Game, Ruy Lopez, Queen's Gambit, London System, Sicilian Defense (Open), Caro-Kann Defense. Those six give a strong foundation for club chess.

chapters.title: Italian Game
chapters.content:
Moves (main idea start): 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4

Core idea: White develops the king's bishop to c4, aiming at Black's weakest square f7, while fighting for the center and preparing to castle quickly. One of the oldest and most natural openings — excellent for teaching principles and tactics.

Main line taught in this app (Giuoco Piano flavor):
1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Bc5 4.c3 Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+

Why key moves:
- e4 / Nf3 / Bc4: center, development, pressure on f7
- c3: prepares the central break d4
- d4: strike in the center while ahead (or equal) in development
- After ...Bb4+: White usually plays Nc3 or Bd2 to meet the check

Typical plans for White:
- Occupy/control the center with pawns and pieces
- Castle kingside early
- Look for tactics around f7 (especially if Black is careless with ...Nxe4 ideas or delayed castling)
- In quieter lines (Giuoco Pianissimo with d3), slowly improve pieces and prepare central or kingside play

What Black wants:
- Mirror development (...Bc5 or ...Nf6)
- Challenge the center and castle safely
- In Two Knights Defense (3...Nf6), sharper play; White must know whether to play calmly or enter Ng5 lines

Common student mistakes:
- Bringing the queen out too early to chase mates that are not there
- Playing d4 too late (or without c3 support) and losing the e4 pawn for nothing
- Ignoring development to hunt pawns

Coaching cues: Celebrate clear development and the c3–d4 idea. Stress that the Italian teaches center + piece activity + f7 awareness.

chapters.title: Ruy Lopez
chapters.content:
Moves: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5

Also called the Spanish Opening. White pins (or pressures) the knight on c6, which helps defend e5 — so White fights for lasting pressure on Black's center. Rich, strategic, and a gold-standard Open Game for improving players.

Main line taught in this app (Morphy / Closed direction):
1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5 a6 4.Ba4 Nf6 5.O-O Be7 6.Re1 b5 7.Bb3 d6

Why key moves:
- Bb5: pressure on the defender of e5; long-term structural ideas
- ...a6: asks the bishop to capture or retreat (Morphy Defense)
- Ba4: keeps the bishop and the pressure
- O-O and Re1: king safety and support of e4
- ...b5 and Bb3: Black gains queenside space; White's bishop stays on a strong diagonal toward f7
- ...d6: solidifies e5 and prepares completing development

Typical plans for White:
- Maintain pressure on e5 and control the center
- Often play c3 and d4 (or a slower d3 setup) depending on the line
- Maneuver knights (classic Closed Spanish ideas include Nbd2–f1–g3)
- Use a space advantage and patient improvement rather than an instant attack

What Black wants:
- Queenside space with ...a6 and ...b5
- Solid development (...Be7, ...d6, castling)
- Counterplay with timely ...exd4, ...c5, or piece activity
- Alternatives early on include the Berlin (3...Nf6), which is very solid

Common student mistakes:
- Capturing on c6 at the wrong time without a clear structural plan
- Ignoring ...Nxe4 ideas when White castles (Open Spanish territory — know the compensation idea)
- Playing too passively and letting Black equalize comfortably without a plan

Coaching cues: Emphasize long-term pressure and piece placement over forcing tactics. The Ruy Lopez rewards patience and understanding of structures.

chapters.title: Queen's Gambit
chapters.content:
Moves: 1.d4 d5 2.c4

Core idea: White offers the c-pawn to lure Black's d-pawn away from the center. It is not a true sacrifice in most lines — White can often regain the pawn — but the real point is superior central control and a lasting space edge.

Main line taught in this app (Queen's Gambit Declined, Orthodox flavor):
1.d4 d5 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Nf6 4.Bg5 Be7 5.e3 O-O 6.Nf3 Nbd7

Why key moves:
- c4: challenges d5 and fights for the center
- ...e6 (QGD): solid; keeps a firm foothold on d5 (but locks in the light-squared bishop for a while)
- Nc3 / Nf6 / Bg5: classical pressure on the d5 complex
- ...Be7: breaks the pin safely
- e3 / Nf3: complete development without creating needless weaknesses
- ...Nbd7: flexible; prepares ...c5 or ...dxc4 ideas and unblocks the c-pawn later

Typical plans for White:
- Pressure d5 and enjoy a space advantage
- Complete development, castle, connect rooks
- In many QGD / Carlsbad structures: minority attack on the queenside (b4–b5) to create a weak c-pawn
- Central breaks or piece pressure when Black is passive

What Black wants:
- A solid structure and safe king
- Free the light-squared bishop eventually (...b6 / ...Bb7 or via exchanges)
- Challenge the center with ...c5 or ...e5 at the right moment
- In Queen's Gambit Accepted (2...dxc4), give up the center temporarily for rapid development — White should reclaim the center with e3/e4 ideas

Common student mistakes:
- Treating 2.c4 as a reckless gambit and never fighting to regain central control
- Moving the queen early instead of developing minor pieces
- Ignoring Black's ...c5 break until it equalizes easily

Coaching cues: Stress that the "gambit" is about the center and structure. Praise solid developing moves and explain space + minority-attack ideas in simple terms.

chapters.title: London System
chapters.content:
Typical setup for White: 1.d4, Nf3, Bf4, e3, c3, Nbd2 (move order flexible)

Core idea: A setup-based opening. White plays a reliable triangle of pawns (c3–d4–e3) and develops the dark-squared bishop outside the pawn chain to f4 before locking it in with e3. Easy to learn, solid, and popular from club level to grandmasters.

Main line taught in this app:
1.d4 d5 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 c5 4.e3 Nc6 5.c3 e6 6.Nbd2 Bd6

Why key moves:
- Bf4: the signature London bishop — active before e3
- e3 and c3: rock-solid support of d4; hard for Black to "break"
- Nbd2: connects development and supports e4 or Nf3 ideas later
- Black's ...c5 and ...Bd6: natural challenges to White's center and the f4 bishop

Typical plans for White:
- Complete development (Bd3, O-O, Qc2 or Qe2)
- Often aim for a knight on e5
- Kingside attacking motifs with Bd3, Qc2, and pressure toward h7 when Black castles short
- Meet ...Bd6 with exchanges or retreat (e.g. Bg3) depending on preference
- Play for e4 in some lines once pieces are ready

What Black wants:
- Challenge d4 with ...c5
- Develop freely and castle
- Trade White's strong f4 bishop or neutralize it
- Avoid passive setups where White gets a free kingside initiative

Common student mistakes:
- Playing the London moves on autopilot without noticing Black's threats
- Forgetting to castle while pushing kingside pawns
- Exchanging the f4 bishop poorly and leaving dark-square holes

Coaching cues: Perfect opening for teaching "setup + plan." Remind students that system openings still require reacting to the opponent — principles still apply.

chapters.title: Sicilian Defense
chapters.content:
Moves: 1.e4 c5

Core idea: Black fights for the center asymmetrically. Instead of mirroring with ...e5, Black uses the c-pawn to control d4 and create unbalanced positions with winning chances for both sides. The most ambitious and popular reply to 1.e4 at many levels.

Open Sicilian idea (White): 2.Nf3 and 3.d4, opening the position to use a development lead.

Main line taught in this app (Najdorf-direction Open Sicilian):
1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 d6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 Nf6 5.Nc3 a6 6.Be2 e5

Why key moves:
- ...c5: asymmetric fight for d4
- d4 / ...cxd4 / Nxd4: Open Sicilian — White opens lines; Black gets a semi-open c-file
- ...Nf6 and Nc3: classic development and e4 defense
- ...a6 (Najdorf move): prepares ...b5 and restricts Nb5 ideas
- Be2: flexible, relatively calm setup for White
- ...e5: Black gains space but weakens d5 — a classic Sicilian trade-off

Typical plans for White:
- Rapid development and king safety
- Use central outposts (often d5) and piece activity
- In sharper systems (e.g. English Attack ideas): f3, g4, queenside castling, pawn storms — teach motifs carefully to beginners
- Punish slow play; Sicilians are concrete

What Black wants:
- Counterplay on the c-file and queenside (...a6, ...b5, ...Qc7, ...Rc8)
- The thematic break ...d5 when prepared
- Dynamic chances even if White has more space or attacking prospects

Common student mistakes (as White):
- Playing timidly and letting Black seize the initiative on the queenside
- Memorizing Najdorf theory instead of learning structures (d5 hole, c-file, development race)
- Ignoring king safety while attacking

Coaching cues: Keep theory light. Teach the Open Sicilian trade (activity vs structure), the c-file, and the ...d5 break. Celebrate energetic developing moves.

chapters.title: Caro-Kann Defense
chapters.content:
Moves: 1.e4 c6 2.d4 d5

Core idea: Black prepares ...d5 with ...c6 so that after the center opens, the light-squared bishop can often develop outside the pawn chain (unlike many French Defense lines where ...e6 locks it in). Solid, respected, and low-risk — excellent for positional students.

Main line taught in this app (Classical flavor from White's side):
1.e4 c6 2.d4 d5 3.Nc3 dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 5.Ng3 Bg6 6.h4 h6

Why key moves:
- ...c6 / ...d5: challenge the center without blocking the light-squared bishop yet
- Nc3 (or Nd2): defend e4; after ...dxe4, recapture with a piece on a strong square
- ...Bf5: the defining Classical idea — bishop outside before ...e6
- Ng3: gains a tempo on the bishop
- ...Bg6 and ...h6: keep the bishop; stop h5 ideas from trapping it
- h4: White probes the bishop and gains kingside space

Typical plans for White:
- Enjoy a space advantage and freer piece play
- Complete development (Bd3, Nf3, O-O or sometimes queenside ideas)
- Pressure Black's slightly more cramped position without overextending
- In Advance Variation (3.e5), play against Black's light squares and space — different structure, same solid Black philosophy

What Black wants:
- A healthy pawn structure and safe king
- Trade toward a comfortable endgame
- The freeing break ...c5 at the right moment
- Neutralize White's space and avoid passive piece placement

Common student mistakes (as White):
- Attacking recklessly against a rock-solid structure
- Allowing Black to equalize easily with ...c5 while White's pieces are uncoordinated
- Underestimating Black's bishop on the b1–h7 diagonal

Coaching cues: Contrast with the French (...e6 blocks the bishop). Praise the logic of ...Bf5. Teach White to use space patiently rather than force tactics that are not there.

chapters.title: Common Opening Mistakes
chapters.content:
Frequent beginner errors and how to coach them:

1. Neglecting development
Symptom: Many pawn moves, same piece moved repeatedly, pieces still on the back rank.
Fix: Ask "which undeveloped piece can enter the game usefully?" Reminder: knights and bishops out, then castle.

2. King left in the center
Symptom: Middlegame begins and the king is still on e1/e8.
Fix: Prioritize castling. Show how open files punish a central king.

3. Early queen adventures
Symptom: Qh5/Qf3/Qa4 very early without a concrete tactic.
Fix: Explain tempo loss when the queen is harassed by developing moves.

4. Ignoring the center
Symptom: Flank pawn pushes while the opponent occupies e4/d4/e5/d5.
Fix: Return to the four central squares; place a pawn or aim a piece there.

5. Grabbing poisoned pawns
Symptom: Taking b2/b7 or similar while undeveloped.
Fix: Prefer development and king safety; calculate only when ahead in development or the grab is safe.

6. Moving without a plan after the book line ends
Symptom: Student finishes the lesson moves and freezes.
Fix: Recite the opening's goals: pawn break, piece outposts, which side to play on.

7. Memorizing moves without reasons
Symptom: Student plays the line but cannot explain why.
Fix: After each move, ask for a one-sentence purpose (develops / defends / prepares a break / threatens).

8. Overreacting to every threat
Symptom: Passive retreats and weakening pawn moves against phantom attacks.
Fix: Teach asking "is it a real threat?" before creating weaknesses.

When the student goes off the lesson main line: name the expected move, briefly say why, and ask them to undo — stay kind and specific.

chapters.title: Pawn Structures and Middlegame Plans
chapters.content:
Openings matter because they create pawn structures — and structures suggest plans.

Open Games (1.e4 e5 — Italian, Ruy Lopez):
- More open lines; piece activity and tactics matter
- Typical themes: fight for d4/d5, pressure on e5/e4, kingside attacks after development
- Plan question: "Where do my pieces go, and which central break opens lines for me?"

Queen's Gambit / classical 1.d4 d5:
- Often semi-closed; long-term structure play
- Carlsbad-type structures: White minority attack (b4–b5) vs Black kingside or central counterplay
- Plan question: "Can I create a weakness on the queenside, or should I play in the center?"

London System:
- Solid pyramid c3–d4–e3; dark-squared bishop on f4
- Plans often revolve around Ne5, Bd3, and kingside pressure
- Plan question: "Is my f4 bishop still useful, and is e4 or a kingside attack ready?"

Open Sicilian:
- Asymmetric: White may attack faster; Black uses c-file and ...d5
- Structural landmarks: hole on d5 (especially after ...e5), semi-open c-file for Black
- Plan question: "Am I racing on opposite wings, or fighting for a central break?"

Caro-Kann:
- Solid Black structure; White has space
- Black seeks ...c5; White tries to use space and piece activity before Black frees the position
- Plan question: "Has Black equalized with ...c5, or am I still squeezing?"

Coaching tip: When a student asks "what now?" after the opening, answer with structure + one plan + one next useful move — not a long variation list.

chapters.title: Algebraic Notation for Coaching
chapters.content:
Use standard algebraic notation (SAN) in text. Speak clearly in audio.

Basics:
- Files a–h left to right from White's perspective; ranks 1–8 from White to Black
- Pieces: K (king), Q (queen), R (rook), B (bishop), N (knight). Pawns have no letter.
- Examples: e4 (pawn to e4), Nf3 (knight to f3), Bb5 (bishop to b5), O-O (castle kingside), O-O-O (castle queenside)
- Captures use x: Nxe5, Bxc6, exd5
- Check: + ; checkmate: #
- Disambiguate when needed: Nbd2, R1e1, Qh4e1

Spoken forms for voice coaching:
- e4 → "e four"
- Nf3 → "knight to f three"
- Bb5 → "bishop to b five"
- O-O → "castle kingside"
- Nxe5 → "knight takes e five"
- ...c5 (Black) → "black plays c five" when clarity helps

Roles in this app:
- Student plays White; coach plays Black
- "You" = student's white move; "I" = coach's black move

Always prefer clear SAN in written hints, and plain spoken language in audio so beginners are not lost.

chapters.title: Lesson Teaching Guidelines
chapters.content:
How to coach inside Chess Opening Trainer lesson mode.

Goals:
- Teach the main-line idea, not encyclopedic theory
- Keep feedback short, warm, and specific
- Use provided hint notes as the pedagogical source of truth when present

On a correct main-line move:
- Brief praise or acknowledgment
- One sentence on why the move is good (principle or plan)
- After your (Black) reply, clearly state the student's next recommended move

On an off-line move:
- Say it is not the lesson main line
- Name the correct move in SAN (and speak it clearly in audio)
- Briefly explain using any hint
- Ask the student to tap undo

On questions ("why?", "what's the plan?"):
- Answer in 3–5 beginner-friendly sentences
- Lead with the idea, then the concrete move if needed
- Fetch a knowledge-base chapter when the question is about opening ideas, plans, or principles beyond the current hint

Tone:
- Encouraging, never sarcastic
- Celebrate good principle-based moves even if imperfect
- Correct mistakes gently and specifically

Do not:
- Dump long variation trees
- Merely recount the board without teaching
- Invent deep theory that contradicts the lesson line or hint notes

When unsure which chapter to use: start with Opening Principles, then the chapter for the current lesson opening (Italian Game, Ruy Lopez, Queen's Gambit, London System, Sicilian Defense, or Caro-Kann Defense).

chapters.title: Free Play Coaching Guidelines
chapters.content:
How to coach in free play (casual games against the coach).

Goals:
- Keep the game fun and instructive
- React to moves with short pedagogical notes
- Reinforce opening principles even outside a fixed lesson line

Early game (opening phase):
- Notice whether the student controls the center, develops, and castles
- If they play a known opening, you may name it and mention its main idea briefly
- Suggest improvements when a clear principle is broken

When the student asks for help:
- Give one concrete suggestion (a move or a plan), with a short reason
- Prefer principles and typical plans over engine-perfect moves
- Use knowledge-base chapters for deeper opening questions

Game over:
- One short explanation of how the game ended
- One encouraging takeaway (a principle or habit to remember next time)

Tone and length:
- 2–4 short sentences for routine move reactions
- Slightly longer only when the student asks for explanation
- Vary phrasing so consecutive turns do not sound identical

Stay in character as a friendly opening coach: patient, clear, and focused on learning.
